slides.title: Computer Fundamentals: The CPU
slides.content: The Central Processing Unit is the 'brain' of the computer, executing instructions stored in memory. In our stack, the CPU handles everything from PHP processing to Go compilation.

slides.title: Computer Fundamentals: Memory (RAM)
slides.content: RAM is volatile storage used for active processes. When you run `server.ts`, Node.js allocates memory for your application state.

slides.title: Computer Fundamentals: Binary and Data
slides.content: Computers process data as 0s and 1s. Every character in `README.md` is encoded in UTF-8, represented by binary sequences.

slides.title: Computer Fundamentals: Storage (SSD/HDD)
slides.content: Persistent storage holds your codebase. Your files in `/repo-php/` live here until loaded into RAM for execution.

slides.title: Computer Fundamentals: The Operating System
slides.content: The OS (like Linux in our Docker container) manages hardware resources. Our `Dockerfile` defines the environment the OS provides.

slides.title: Computer Fundamentals: Networking (DNS)
slides.content: Domain Name System translates human names like site1.com into IP addresses. Our local environment uses `nginx.conf` to mimic this routing.

slides.title: Computer Fundamentals: HTTP Protocol
slides.content: The web runs on Request and Response. PHP receives a GET request and sends back HTML in `repo-php/public/index.php`.

slides.title: Computer Fundamentals: Ports and Proxies
slides.content: Nginx listens on port 80 and proxies requests to our PHP or Node servers. Check `docker-compose.yml` for port mappings.

slides.title: Computer Fundamentals: Virtualization
slides.content: Docker allows us to run isolated containers. This ensures 'it works on my machine' applies to the production server too.

slides.title: Computer Fundamentals: The Kernel
slides.content: The core of the OS that interfaces directly with hardware. Docker containers share the host's kernel but have their own user space.

slides.title: Program Fundamentals: Algorithms
slides.content: An algorithm is a step-by-step procedure for solving a problem. `Router.php` uses an algorithm to match URLs to file paths.

slides.title: Program Fundamentals: Variables and Types
slides.content: Variables store data. TypeScript adds strict typing to ensure a variable intended for a number isn't assigned a string.

slides.title: Program Fundamentals: Control Structures
slides.content: If/Else and Loops control the flow of a program. See `View.php` to see how it loops through component data.

slides.title: Program Fundamentals: Functions
slides.content: Functions are reusable blocks of code. `DB.php` contains functions to query the database efficiently.

slides.title: Program Fundamentals: Data Structures (Arrays)
slides.content: Arrays store lists of items. Our `sites.json` uses an array structure to list all available websites.

slides.title: Program Fundamentals: Data Structures (Objects/Maps)
slides.content: Objects (or associative arrays in PHP) store key-value pairs. `config.json` is a perfect example of this.

slides.title: Program Fundamentals: Compilation
slides.content: Compiled languages like Go or Rust transform code into machine-executable binaries before running.

slides.title: Program Fundamentals: Interpretation
slides.content: Interpreted languages like PHP or Python execute code line-by-line via an interpreter at runtime.

slides.title: Program Fundamentals: Recursion
slides.content: A function calling itself. Useful for traversing hierarchical structures like the CMS folder tree in `Hierarchy.php`.

slides.title: Program Fundamentals: Error Handling
slides.content: Try/Catch blocks prevent your app from crashing when something goes wrong. Essential for database connections in `DB.php`.

slides.title: Software Engineering: The SDLC
slides.content: The Software Development Life Cycle includes Planning, Analysis, Design, Implementation, and Maintenance.

slides.title: Software Engineering: Version Control (Git)
slides.content: Git tracks changes to code over time. Every file in this project is part of a Git repository history.

slides.title: Software Engineering: Agile Methodology
slides.content: Agile focuses on iterative development and constant feedback. We use small modules in our training to mirror this.

slides.title: Software Engineering: Technical Debt
slides.content: Fast but 'dirty' fixes accumulate debt. Refactoring `repo-php` into modern stacks like `repo-react` reduces this debt.

slides.title: Software Engineering: CI/CD
slides.content: Continuous Integration and Deployment automate testing and pushing code to production. Our `Dockerfile` is the basis for CI.

slides.title: Software Engineering: Documentation
slides.content: Good code needs good docs. The `/docs/` folder in this repo is vital for understanding the complex CMS architecture.

slides.title: Software Engineering: Code Reviews
slides.content: Peer review improves code quality and shares knowledge. It is the human equivalent of running `eslint`.

slides.title: Software Engineering: Testing Pyramid
slides.content: A mix of Unit, Integration, and E2E tests ensures stability. We test small components in `/repo-php/plugins/`.

slides.title: Software Engineering: Requirement Engineering
slides.content: Understanding WHAT to build before HOW to build it. `metadata.json` defines the core requirements for each applet.

slides.title: Software Engineering: Maintainability
slides.content: Writing code that others (or future you) can easily update. Clean class structures in `repo-php` promote this.

slides.title: Design Patterns: The Singleton
slides.content: Ensures a class has only one instance. `DB.php` often uses this pattern to manage a single shared database connection.

slides.title: Design Patterns: Factory Method
slides.content: Creates objects without specifying the exact class. `Component.php` uses this to generate different UI elements dynamically.

slides.title: Design Patterns: Observer Pattern
slides.content: An object maintains a list of dependents and notifies them of state changes. Vue's reactivity in `src/App.vue` uses this.

slides.title: Design Patterns: MVC (Model-View-Controller)
slides.content: Separates data (Model), logic (Controller), and UI (View). `repo-php` is architected entirely around this separation.

slides.title: Design Patterns: Strategy Pattern
slides.content: Defines a family of algorithms and makes them interchangeable. Our `AIAgent.php` selects different prompts based on intent.

slides.title: Design Patterns: Decorator Pattern
slides.content: Adds behavior to an object dynamically. Plugins in `repo-php/plugins/` act as decorators for the core CMS functionality.

slides.title: Design Patterns: Adapter Pattern
slides.content: Allows incompatible interfaces to work together. We use this to bridge `repo-php` data into `repo-react` frontend.

slides.title: Design Patterns: Repository Pattern
slides.content: Mediates between the domain and data mapping layers. used in `Store.php` to abstract file system storage.

slides.title: Design Patterns: Command Pattern
slides.content: Encapsulates a request as an object. `AITaskManager.php` treats AI requests as executable tasks in a queue.

slides.title: Design Patterns: Dependency Injection
slides.content: Objects receive other objects they depend on. PHP's `App.php` injects the Router into the main execution flow.

slides.title: UI Design: Visual Hierarchy
slides.content: Guiding the user's eye to the most important elements first. Headlines in `onepage.demo/index.php` use size and weight for this.

slides.title: UI Design: Consistency
slides.content: Using recurring patterns for buttons, colors, and fonts. `style.css` defines standard Tailwind classes for our components.

slides.title: UI Design: Typography
slides.content: The art of arranging type to make written language legible and appealing. We use sans-serif fonts for a modern, clean look.

slides.title: UI Design: Color Theory
slides.content: Using color to evoke emotion and provide feedback. Red for errors, green for success in `AdminSubmissions.js`.

slides.title: UI Design: Layout (Flexbox/Grid)
slides.content: Systematic arrangements of elements on a page. Our responsive sidebars use CSS Flexbox to adapt to mobile screens.

slides.title: UI Design: White Space
slides.content: Also known as negative space, it prevents clutter and allows content to 'breathe'. Used extensively in `startup.demo`.

slides.title: UI Design: Affordance
slides.content: Visual cues that suggest how an element should be used. Buttons in `CmsButton.js` look clickable because of shadows and borders.

slides.title: UI Design: Responsive Breakpoints
slides.content: Specific widths where layout changes. Tailwind classes like `md:`, `lg:` help us manage this in `index.html`.

slides.title: UI Design: Icons and Imagery
slides.content: Visual symbols that convey meaning quickly. SVG icons in our dashboards provide context without text overhead.

slides.title: UI Design: Dark Mode
slides.content: A high-contrast theme that reduces eye strain. Modern app architectures often support theme toggles via CSS variables.

slides.title: UX Design: User Research
slides.content: Understanding the needs and pain points of your users BEFORE building. Vital for our CMS admin interface design.

slides.title: UX Design: User Flows
slides.content: The path a user takes through your app to complete a task. `AdminRouter.js` manages these transitions in the admin panel.

slides.title: UX Design: Accessibility (A11y)
slides.content: Ensuring everyone can use your software, including those with disabilities. Use `alt` tags and semantic HTML.

slides.title: UX Design: Information Architecture
slides.content: Organizing data so it makes sense to the user. Our sidebar categorizes tools into 'Dashboard', 'Editor', and 'Sync'.

slides.title: UX Design: Feedback Loops
slides.content: Letting the user know the system received their input. Loading spinners and success toasts in `AdminAiTasks.js` provide this.

slides.title: UX Design: Friction
slides.content: Removing unnecessary steps to make a process smoother. Our form plugin aims for 'Zero-Config' setup.

slides.title: UX Design: Error Prevention
slides.content: Designing UI so errors are hard to make. Dropdowns instead of text for 'status' in `AdminFormsManager.js` help here.

slides.title: UX Design: Cognitive Load
slides.content: The amount of mental effort required to use your app. Clean layouts in `babiblog.fr` keep this low for readers.

slides.title: UX Design: Micro-copy
slides.content: Short bits of text like button labels and tooltips. 'Deploy' vs 'Save' can change the user's expectation.

slides.title: UX Design: Prototype Testing
slides.content: Testing low-fidelity versions of a feature to validate ideas. This repo started as a 'onpage.demo' prototype.

slides.title: DX (Developer Experience): Tooling
slides.content: The ecosystem that developers use. VS Code, npm, and Vite create the 'DX loop' for this project.

slides.title: DX: Scaffolding and CLIs
slides.content: Tools that generate boilerplate code. `npm create vite` is a classic example used for our React/Vue repos.

slides.title: DX: IDE Support
slides.content: IntelliSense and Type checking (via TypeScript) help developers find bugs as they type them.

slides.title: DX: Faster Feedback Loops
slides.content: Hot Module Replacement (HMR) allows code changes to appear instantly without a full page refresh.

slides.title: DX: API Documentation
slides.content: Clean, searchable documentation makes integrating systems easier. check our `/docs/` for API examples.

slides.title: DX: Consistent Code Style
slides.content: Using Linters (Eslint) and Formatters (Prettier) to keep the codebase uniform for all contributors.

slides.title: DX: Dependency Management
slides.content: Managing external libraries cleanly. `package.json` tracks versions so others can reproduce our build environment.

slides.title: DX: Sandbox Environments
slides.content: Local development setups that mimic production. Our `docker-compose.yml` provides this environment perfectly.

slides.title: DX: Error Messaging
slides.content: Clear, actionable error logs. The 'Insufficient permissions' errors in our Firebase skill are designed to be caught and handled.

slides.title: DX: Automated Refactoring
slides.content: Using scripts or AI agents to clean up code. `docs/ai-agents/ai-refactoring-algorithm.md` outlines our approach.

slides.title: AX (AI Experience): The AI Coder Agent
slides.content: Designing software specifically for AI agents to understand and modify. Clean, modular code is high 'AX' code.

slides.title: AX: Prompt Engineering for Developers
slides.content: The art of writing instructions that an AI can execute reliably. See our `AI-STUDENT-PROMPTS.md` for examples.

slides.title: AX: Human-in-the-Loop
slides.content: AI generates the code, but the human reviews and integrates it. This is the heart of the FRAISE approach.

slides.title: AX: Structured Metadata for Agents
slides.content: Providing JSON context (like `metadata.json`) so the AI knows the project's boundaries and capabilities.

slides.title: AX: Self-Correcting Code
slides.content: Agents that can detect errors and fix them automatically. This requires clear integration points and error logs.

slides.title: AX: Grounding with LLMs
slides.content: Connecting AI agents to real documentation and files to prevent hallucinations. Our `docs/` folder 'grounds' the agent.

slides.title: AX: Continuous Training for Agents
slides.content: Keeping agent instructions (in `AGENTS.md`) up to date with new project patterns.

slides.title: AX: Multi-Agent Workflows
slides.content: One agent writes code, another writes tests, a third generates media. `media-generation-toolchain.md` explains this.

slides.title: AX: AI Privacy and Data Security
slides.content: Ensuring sensitive keys aren't leaked to LLMs. We use `.env.example` to hide secrets from the codebase.

slides.title: AX: Future of Software Engineering
slides.content: The shift from writing code to managing logic flows and agent instructions. You are at the forefront of this shift.

slides.title: OOP: Classes and Objects
slides.content: A class is a blueprint; an object is an instance. `AIAgent.php` is a class used to create specialized AI agents.

slides.title: OOP: Encapsulation
slides.content: Hiding internal data and exposing only what is necessary. `CMS.php` encapsulates the complexity of site management.

slides.title: OOP: Inheritance
slides.content: A class acquiring properties of another. Our `Controller.php` provides base functionality that other controllers inherit.

slides.title: OOP: Polymorphism
slides.content: One interface, many forms. Different plugins in `/plugins/` all implement a standard interface but behave differently.

slides.title: OOP: Composition over Inheritance
slides.content: Building complex objects by combining smaller ones. `Layout.php` composes headers, sidebars, and main content.

slides.title: OOP: Abstraction
slides.content: Generalizing behavior to simplify usage. `Model.php` abstracts database queries so you don't write SQL everywhere.

slides.title: OOP: SOLID Principles (S)
slides.content: Single Responsibility Principle: A class should have only one reason to change. `Email.php` (if it existed) shouldn't format PDFs.

slides.title: OOP: Interfaces
slides.content: Contracts that define what a class must do. `Component.php` forces all UI parts to have a `render()` method.

slides.title: OOP: Static Methods
slides.content: Methods that belong to the class itself, not an instance. Often used for 'helper' functions in `App.php`.

slides.title: OOP: Constructors
slides.content: Special functions that run when an object is created. `Router.php` initializes its routing table in the constructor.

slides.title: Security: Input Sanitization
slides.content: Always cleaning user data to prevent XSS and SQL injection. Crucial in `FormsManager.php`.

slides.title: Security: Authentication
slides.content: Verifying who a user is. Firebase Auth (discussed in our skill) is a industry standard for this project.

slides.title: Security: Authorization (RBAC)
slides.content: Controlling what a user can do. Our admin panel uses role-based access to restrict specific dashboards.

slides.title: Security: Encryption (At Rest)
slides.content: Storing data securely on disk. SQLite databases or JSON files should be protected by file-system permissions.

slides.title: Security: HTTPS/SSL
slides.content: Encrypting data as it travels over the web. Our Nginx config ensures traffic is secure when deployed via Docker.

slides.title: Testing: Unit Testing
slides.content: Testing individual functions in isolation. Vitest or Jest are commonly used in the modern repos like `repo-react`.

slides.title: Testing: Integration Testing
slides.content: Testing how different modules work together. Like testing if the `PluginManager` loads the `Forms` plugin correctly.

slides.title: Testing: E2E (End-to-End)
slides.content: Simulating a real user journey through the browser. Tools like Playwright or Cypress verify the full stack.

slides.title: Security: OWASP Top 10
slides.content: A list of the 10 most critical web application security risks. Understanding these is vital for any full-stack dev.

slides.title: The Principle of Code Reuse
slides.content: Code reuse is the practice of using existing code for new software. In our project, the `repo-php/class/` directory serves as a library of reusable logic used across multiple demo sites.

slides.title: DRY: Don't Repeat Yourself
slides.content: The DRY principle aims to reduce repetition of software patterns. If you find yourself copying code between `site1.com` and `site2.com`, it should probably be moved to a shared component or class.

slides.title: Understanding Libraries
slides.content: A library is a collection of pre-written code that you can call. You are in control: you decide when to call the library. Example: `d3` for data visualization in our stack.

slides.title: Package Managers: NPM and Composer
slides.content: Package managers automate the process of installing, upgrading, and configuring libraries. Our `package.json` manages JavaScript dependencies for the entire workspace.

slides.title: Understanding Frameworks
slides.content: A framework provides a foundation for building applications. Unlike a library, a framework calls your code (Inversion of Control). Our `repo-react` and `repo-vue` folders demonstrate different framework architectures.

slides.title: The Role of a CMS
slides.content: A Content Management System (CMS) allows non-technical users to manage website content. Our `repo-php` is a custom CMS that stores content in structured PHP files.

slides.title: Headless vs Traditional CMS
slides.content: Traditional CMS (like our PHP demos) handles both content and presentation. A Headless CMS provides content via an API, which can then be consumed by frameworks like React.

slides.title: Decoupled Architectures
slides.content: In a decoupled system, the backend (CMS) and frontend are independent. This allows us to use PHP for content management and React for the user interface.

slides.title: Open Source: A Collaborative Engine
slides.content: Open Source software has its source code available for anyone to inspect, modify, and enhance. Most of the tools we use (Node, PHP, Linux) are Open Source.

slides.title: Open Source Licensing
slides.content: Licenses like MIT, Apache, and GPL define how code can be reused. Our project follows open principles to ensure developers can learn from and extend the architecture.

slides.title: Contributing to the Ecosystem
slides.content: Contributing isn't just writing code; it's also reporting bugs, improving documentation, and helping others. The `docs/` folder in this repo is a primary area for contribution.

slides.title: The Proprietary vs Open Source Debate
slides.content: Open source offers transparency and community support, while proprietary software often provides specialized support and controlled environments. We prioritize Open Source for maximum flexibility.

slides.title: Software Engineering: Lifetime Learning
slides.content: The field changes daily. Being a great engineer means never stopping your education. Congrats on finishing these 112 fundamentals!
